optionExplanations.D: Throwing an exception from the tool handler breaks the MCP protocol and puts error handling in the framework layer rather than surfacing the error as structured data for the model to interpret.

questions.title: process_refund Timeout: Agent Has Context but Cannot Execute Refund
questions.text: During a billing dispute resolution, your agent successfully retrieves customer info via get_customer and order details via lookup_order, but when attempting to call process_refund, the tool returns a timeout error. The agent has enough information to explain the charges and verify refund eligibility, but cannot actually process the refund due to the backend failure. What approach best balances first-contact resolution with appropriate error handling?

options.A: Explain the billing, confirm refund eligibility, acknowledge the system issue preventing immediate processing, and offer escalation or retry later
options.B: Implement automatic retries with exponential backoff for process_refund, keeping the conversation open until the refund is successfully processed
options.C: Confirm the refund will be processed and close the conversation, since the system has all necessary information to complete it automatically
options.D: Escalate immediately to a human agent since the refund action cannot be completed

questions.explanation:
Why A: The agent provides maximum value with available information, is transparent about the technical
limitation, and gives the customer actionable next steps. Blocking on retries (B) holds the customer hostage to an
uncertain backend recovery.

optionExplanations.A: The agent provides maximum value with available information, is transparent about the technical limitation, and gives the customer actionable next steps. Blocking on retries (B) holds the customer hostage to an uncertain backend recovery.
optionExplanations.B: Automatic retries with exponential backoff can trap the customer in a prolonged conversation waiting for an uncertain backend recovery, wasting their time when escalation is more appropriate.
optionExplanations.C: Confirming refund processing without actually completing it is misleading to the customer and may create false expectations about when money will arrive.
optionExplanations.D: Immediate escalation skips the opportunity to provide transparency about what was verified and why human help is needed, reducing the value of the work already completed.

questions.title: Escalation Handoff After 25+ Turns: $847 Refund
questions.text: After investigating a billing dispute over 25+ turns, you've identified that duplicate charges occurred due to a payment gateway timeout triggering retry logic. The required refund ($847) exceeds your $500 authorization limit. You need to call escalate_to_human, and the human agent won't have access to your conversation transcript. What context should you pass to enable effective resolution?

options.A: A structured summary: customer ID, root cause, refund amount, and recommended action.
options.B: The customer's original complaint verbatim plus the tool result excerpts showing duplicate transactions.
options.C: Your diagnosis and the refund amount only.
options.D: The complete conversation transcript with all tool results.

questions.explanation:
Why A: A synthesized structured summary is what the human agent needs to act quickly. The complete
transcript (D) is too much; only the diagnosis (C) is too little.

optionExplanations.A: A synthesized structured summary is what the human agent needs to act quickly. The complete transcript (D) is too much; only the diagnosis (C) is too little.
optionExplanations.B: Including the customer's verbatim complaint and tool excerpts forces the human agent to parse raw, unprocessed data when they need actionable context; this adds cognitive overhead rather than enabling quick resolution.
optionExplanations.C: Omitting the customer ID and recommended action leaves the agent without key identifiers and decision guidance, making it impossible to act efficiently on the refund.
optionExplanations.D: The complete transcript exceeds what the agent needs and violates the stated constraint that they won't have transcript access; it also buries critical facts in noise.

questions.title: Customer Raises 3 Issues in One Session (Turns 1–45), Context Approaching
questions.text: A customer raises three separate issues during one session: a refund inquiry (turns 1-15), a subscription question (turns 16-30), and a payment method update (turns 31-45). At turn 48, the customer asks "What happened with my refund?" The conversation is approaching context limits. What strategy best maintains the agent's ability to address all issues throughout the session?

options.A: Rely on MCP tools to re-fetch relevant information on demand when the customer references earlier issues.
options.B: Implement sliding window context that retains the most recent 30 turns.
options.C: Extract and persist structured issue data (order IDs, amounts, statuses) into a separate context layer.
options.D: Summarize earlier turns into a narrative description, preserving full message history only for the active issue.

questions.explanation:
Why C: A structured data layer persists the key facts from all three issues independently of the conversation
window, so any issue can be referenced accurately at any point in the session.

optionExplanations.A: Relying on on-demand tool fetches risks tool failures, stale cached data, or API timeouts when the customer references old issues; it doesn't proactively preserve the information.
optionExplanations.B: A sliding window of 30 turns may drop references to issues from turns 1–15 before turn 48, making earlier context inaccessible when the customer asks about them.
optionExplanations.C: A structured data layer persists the key facts from all three issues independently of the conversation window, so any issue can be referenced accurately at any point in the session.
optionExplanations.D: A narrative summary is unstructured and prone to loss of precision (exact amounts, dates, order IDs) and makes it harder for the agent to query or update individual issue statuses independently.

questions.title: Customer Returns 4 Hours Later with Stale Tool Data
questions.text: A customer returns 4 hours after their initial session about the same billing dispute. The previous 32-turn session contains lookup_order results showing "Status: PENDING, Expected resolution: 24-48 hours." In testing, you observe that when resuming sessions with stale tool results, the agent often references the outdated data in responses (e.g., "I see your refund is still being processed") even after subsequent fresh tool calls return different information. What approach most reliably handles returning customers?

options.A: Resume with full history and add a system prompt instruction telling the agent to always prefer the most recent tool results when multiple calls to the same tool exist in context.
options.B: Resume with full history and configure the agent to automatically re-call all previously-used tools at session start to ensure data freshness.
options.C: Resume with full history but filter out previous tool_result messages before resuming, keeping only the human/assistant turns so the agent must re-fetch needed data.
options.D: Start a new session, inject a structured summary of the previous interaction (issue type, actions taken, resolution status), then make fresh tool calls before engaging.

questions.explanation:
Why D: Starting fresh with a summary and fresh data guarantees no stale results can influence responses.
Option A still leaves stale data in context and relies on the model to correctly prioritize the newer results.

optionExplanations.A: Still leaves stale data in context and relies on the model to correctly prioritize the newer results.
optionExplanations.B: Automatically re-calling all previously-used tools at session start is wasteful and doesn't solve the core problem: stale results remain in the conversation history and can still mislead the agent if it cherry-picks older data.
optionExplanations.C: Filtering out previous tool_result messages removes evidence of what was already checked, but the agent may still reference stale facts mentioned in its own prior responses or user messages without realizing they're outdated.
optionExplanations.D: Starting fresh with a summary and fresh data guarantees no stale results can influence responses

questions.title: Inconsistent Error Handling Due to Uniform Error Responses
questions.text: Production logs reveal inconsistent error handling: when lookup_order fails, the agent sometimes retries 5+ times (wasteful when the order ID doesn't exist), sometimes escalates immediately (premature for temporary network issues), and sometimes asks users for clarification (inappropriate when the issue is a backend permission error). Investigation shows your MCP tool returns uniform error responses: {"isError": true, "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Operation failed"}]}. The agent cannot distinguish between error types. What's the most effective improvement?

options.A: Implement retry logic with exponential backoff in your MCP server for all errors, returning to the agent only after retries are exhausted.
options.B: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating how to interpret error message patterns and select appropriate responses for each.
options.C: Enhance error responses with structured metadata: include errorCategory (transient/validation/permission), isRetryable boolean, and a description of what caused the failure.
options.D: Create an analyze_error MCP tool the agent calls after any failure to determine the error category and recommended action.

questions.explanation:
Why C: Structured error metadata gives the agent exactly the information it needs to choose the right response
strategy for each type of failure without any ambiguity.

optionExplanations.A: Retry logic with exponential backoff at the server level doesn't help the agent decide whether to retry, escalate, or ask the user—it only addresses transient network failures, not validation or permission errors.
optionExplanations.B: Few-shot examples in the system prompt rely on the model's ability to infer error intent from plain text, which is fragile and fails when error messages are ambiguous or the failure reason differs from past examples.
optionExplanations.C: Structured error metadata gives the agent exactly the information it needs to choose the right response strategy for each type of failure without any ambiguity.
optionExplanations.D: A dedicated analyze_error tool adds latency and an extra call for every failure; the agent still needs to know upfront which errors are retryable versus which require escalation, which only structured metadata provides.

questions.title: How Does the Agentic Loop Choose Next Tool After lookup_order Returns
questions.text: When the agent calls lookup_order and receives order details showing the item was purchased 45 days ago, how does the agentic loop determine whether to call process_refund or escalate_to_human next?

options.A: The orchestration layer automatically routes to the next tool based on the order's status field.
options.B: The agent executes the remaining steps in a tool sequence planned at the start of the request.
options.C: The agent follows a pre-configured decision tree mapping order attributes to specific tool calls.
options.D: The order details are added to the conversation and the model reasons about which action to take.

questions.explanation:
Why D: This is how Claude's agentic loops work. Tool results become part of the conversation context as
tool_result messages, and the model uses those results plus the system prompt policy to reason about the
next step.

optionExplanations.A: This describes a static routing mechanism based on a pre-defined status field, but agentic loops require dynamic reasoning that adapts based on the actual context and policy considerations present in the conversation.
optionExplanations.B: This assumes a rigid pre-planned sequence of tools, but agentic loops are iterative—the model decides what to do next based on each tool result, not a predetermined sequence established upfront.
optionExplanations.C: This relies on a fixed decision tree mapping, but agentic reasoning is flexible and contextual; the model evaluates the specific situation and policies in the conversation rather than following predetermined branching logic.
optionExplanations.D: This is how Claude's agentic loops work. Tool results become part of the conversation context as tool_result messages, and the model uses those results plus the system prompt policy to reason about the next step.

questions.title: MCP Servers from Three Vendors: readOnlyHint vs. destructiveHint Annotations
questions.text: Your marketing agent connects to MCP servers from three different vendors: an email service, a social media platform, and an analytics dashboard. Each server provides tool annotations—some tools marked with readOnlyHint: true, others with destructiveHint: true. Your team proposes automatically bypassing confirmation prompts for tools annotated as read-only to improve workflow speed. What should guide this decision?

options.A: Implement a verification layer that tests each tool's actual behavior before adding it to an allowlist. Skip confirmation only for tools validated through behavioral testing.
options.B: MCP servers run as local processes, so tools from any properly initialized connection inherit your application's security context and can be trusted regardless of vendor.
options.C: Treat tool annotations as untrusted metadata unless the server itself is trusted. Base confirmation requirements on your assessment of each vendor's trustworthiness, not on their self-reported annotations.
options.D: Read-only annotations indicate intended behavior and are reliable for tools that perform GET requests to external APIs. Skip confirmation for data-fetching tools from properly connected servers.

questions.explanation:
Why C: A server can annotate any destructive tool as read-only. Annotations are self-reported metadata, not
independently verifiable. Trust the vendor, not the annotation.

optionExplanations.A: Testing a tool's actual behavior only confirms what it does now, not whether the vendor might annotate a destructive tool as read-only in the future or whether the annotation is trustworthy in the first place.
optionExplanations.B: A tool running locally doesn't prevent the vendor from falsely annotating destructive operations as read-only—the server's location doesn't validate the truthfulness of its self-reported metadata.
optionExplanations.C: A server can annotate any destructive tool as read-only. Annotations are self-reported metadata, not independently verifiable. Trust the vendor, not the annotation.
optionExplanations.D: A read-only annotation is just metadata the server claims about itself; it provides no independent verification that a tool actually only reads data, regardless of what HTTP method it uses.

questions.title: MCP Server vs. Custom Tool for Weather API (Multiple Apps Need Weather Data)
questions.text: You're deciding whether to expose your weather API integration as an MCP server or implement it as a custom tool directly in your agent application. Your team plans to build several different AI applications that will all need weather data. What is the primary advantage of the MCP approach?

options.A: Any MCP-compatible client can connect to your weather server without writing custom integration code.
options.B: MCP servers process tool calls faster than custom implementations because they use an optimized binary protocol.
options.C: MCP provides built-in retry logic that automatically handles failed API calls with exponential backoff.
options.D: MCP automatically handles authentication and rate limiting with the weather API, reducing backend implementation work.

questions.explanation:
Why A: The primary value of MCP is standardized reusability—build the integration once, and any
MCP-compatible client (current or future) can use it. MCP does not automatically handle auth or rate limiting (D).

optionExplanations.A: The primary value of MCP is standardized reusability—build the integration once, and any MCP-compatible client (current or future) can use it. MCP does not automatically handle auth or rate limiting (D).
optionExplanations.B: MCP's advantage is not about protocol speed; the benefit is standardized integration regardless of performance characteristics.
optionExplanations.C: MCP is a protocol for client-server communication—it doesn't include built-in retry logic or exponential backoff for failed API calls; those concerns belong to the server implementation.
optionExplanations.D: MCP does not automatically handle authentication or rate limiting; those tasks remain the responsibility of the server implementation and cannot be delegated to the protocol itself.

questions.title: post_content Confirmation: 98% Approved in 2 Seconds, But Incidents Occur
questions.text: Your post_content tool requires user confirmation before publishing. The current workflow displays "Ready to post to social media. Confirm?" and analytics show users approve 98% of requests within 2 seconds. Post-mortems reveal incidents where posts went to wrong accounts, were scheduled for wrong times, or contained errors—all confirmed by users without catching the mistakes. How should you redesign the confirmation workflow?

options.A: Include the complete post text, target account, scheduled time, and platform in the confirmation request
options.B: Auto-approve routine posts and only require explicit confirmation for unusual patterns like posting to new accounts or large audiences
options.C: Add a mandatory waiting period before the confirm option becomes available
options.D: Require users to type a confirmation phrase instead of clicking a button

questions.explanation:
Why A: Users were rubber-stamping confirmations because the confirmation didn't show them what they were
approving. Making the full details visible in the confirmation surfaces the errors before they happen.

optionExplanations.A: Users were rubber-stamping confirmations because the confirmation didn't show them what they were approving. Making the full details visible in the confirmation surfaces the errors before they happen.
optionExplanations.B: Auto-approving routine posts removes human oversight entirely on those transactions, which is exactly how the original errors occurred—users need to verify details, not bypass confirmation for certain categories.
optionExplanations.C: A waiting period doesn't address the root cause; users were still rubber-stamping confirmations even when given the chance, because they weren't seeing what they were actually approving.
optionExplanations.D: Requiring a typed phrase adds friction but doesn't solve the underlying problem—users can still type a confirmation phrase without reading or understanding the details of what they're confirming.

questions.title: search_documents Tool: Database Selection Parameter Design
questions.text: Your search_documents tool needs a parameter to specify which database to search. Your organization has three document databases: "research_papers", "internal_reports", and "technical_specs". Users express this naturally in conversation ("search the research database", "check technical documents"). How should you design the database selection parameter?

options.A: A freeform string parameter where the backend uses semantic matching to determine which database(s) to search.
options.B: No explicit parameter—search all three databases by default, then have the model filter results by source.
options.C: An enum parameter with values ["research_papers", "internal_reports", "technical_specs"], requiring the model to map natural language to the appropriate value.
options.D: A freeform string parameter with runtime validation that returns an error if the value doesn't match a known database.

questions.explanation:
Why C: Enum parameters are ideal here. The model reliably maps natural language to enum values, and the
parameter prevents invalid values at the schema level.

optionExplanations.A: Semantic matching in the backend introduces ambiguity and is error-prone when determining which of three distinct databases to query; the model should make this decision explicitly upfront.
optionExplanations.B: Searching all three databases by default wastes computational resources and returns irrelevant results; filtering results after retrieval is less efficient than directing the query to the correct database initially.
optionExplanations.C: Enum parameters are ideal here. The model reliably maps natural language to enum values, and the parameter prevents invalid values at the schema level.
optionExplanations.D: Runtime validation only catches errors after the model has already made an incorrect choice, resulting in failed calls; enum validation prevents invalid choices at the schema level before execution.

questions.title: archive_file vs delete_file: Agent Calls Wrong Tool for "Remove Old Backups"
questions.text: Your MCP server includes archive_file(file_id) and delete_file(file_id) tools. Production logs show the agent calls delete_file when users ask to "remove old backups," but company policy requires archiving backup files. Both tools currently have minimal descriptions: "Archives a file" and "Deletes a file." Which change most directly improves tool selection for this scenario?

options.A: Implement server-side validation that rejects delete_file calls for files tagged as backups, returning an error message suggesting archive_file.
options.B: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating that requests involving "backup" or "old" should use archive_file.
options.C: Add a confirmation step that requires users to type "CONFIRM DELETE" before delete_file executes.
options.D: Expand tool descriptions to clarify use cases, adding guidance like "Do not use for backup files" to delete_file.

questions.explanation:
Why D: The model makes tool selection decisions based on descriptions. Clear, accurate descriptions with
explicit guidance about use cases directly solve the tool selection problem at the source.

optionExplanations.A: Server-side validation is a safety net that prevents harm, but it doesn't improve the model's ability to make the correct tool selection decision in the first place.
optionExplanations.B: Few-shot examples help with output format consistency, but they don't directly address the root cause—the model lacks clear information about when each tool should be used.
optionExplanations.C: User confirmation steps occur after the wrong tool has already been selected, adding friction without solving the tool selection problem at the source where the model makes its decision.
optionExplanations.D: The model makes tool selection decisions based on descriptions. Clear, accurate descriptions with explicit guidance about use cases directly solve the tool selection problem at the source.

questions.title: get_portfolio_value: Structured JSON vs. Formatted Text String
questions.text: Your get_portfolio_value tool returns the total value of a user's investment portfolio. You're deciding between returning a structured JSON object with explicit fields versus returning the information as a formatted text string. What is the primary advantage of using structured output with defined fields?

options.A: Structured JSON is processed deterministically by the model, significantly improving accuracy when extracting values.
options.B: Structured JSON consumes significantly fewer tokens than natural language, substantially reducing API costs.
options.C: The agent can reliably extract specific values without parsing free-form text, reducing errors in subsequent operations.
options.D: JSON schemas automatically validate that the underlying API returned correct data before the agent processes it.

questions.explanation:
Why C: The primary advantage is reliable, error-free data access in subsequent agent steps. Free-form text
requires error-prone parsing; structured fields are directly accessible.

optionExplanations.A: Structured JSON doesn't change how the model processes information internally; accuracy gains come from reliable extraction by downstream code, not from the model's processing mechanism.
optionExplanations.B: Token consumption differences between JSON and natural language are minimal and not the primary advantage of structured output in this context.
optionExplanations.C: The primary advantage is reliable, error-free data access in subsequent agent steps. Free-form text requires error-prone parsing; structured fields are directly accessible.
optionExplanations.D: JSON schemas validate the structure of the model's output, not the correctness of data returned by external APIs; API validation is a separate concern from structured output benefits.

questions.title: remove_team_member dry_run Parameter Bypassed in 15% of Cases
questions.text: Your remove_team_member tool uses a dry_run: boolean parameter for previewing impacts before execution. Production monitoring shows the agent bypasses the preview step in 15% of cases by calling with dry_run=false directly. You need to ensure every removal is preceded by a preview that the user explicitly confirms. What is the most reliable approach?

options.A: Add server-side validation that permits dry_run=false only when a dry_run=true call with identical parameters occurred within the past 60 seconds.
options.B: Add detailed instructions and few-shot examples to the tool description requiring the agent to always call with dry_run=true first and wait for user confirmation before calling with dry_run=false.
options.C: Replace with two tools: preview_remove_member returns impact details and a single-use confirmation token; execute_remove_member requires that token, binding execution to the specific previewed action.
options.D: Annotate the tool as requiring confirmation and configure the orchestration layer to prompt the user for approval before forwarding any calls to annotated tools.

questions.explanation:
Why C: The single-use confirmation token structurally enforces the preview→confirm sequence. It is
architecturally impossible to skip the preview, regardless of how the agent is prompted.

optionExplanations.A: This approach depends on the agent remembering to call dry_run=true first and doing so within a narrow time window; it's easily circumvented and doesn't structurally enforce the workflow.
optionExplanations.B: Instructions and examples influence model behavior through prompting, which is fragile and can be overridden by changing model versions, system prompts, or agent reasoning patterns.
optionExplanations.C: The single-use confirmation token structurally enforces the preview→confirm sequence. It is architecturally impossible to skip the preview, regardless of how the agent is prompted.
optionExplanations.D: Orchestration-layer prompts for user approval still allow the agent to call the tool directly in subsequent attempts; there's no structural barrier preventing the agent from bypassing the confirmation workflow.

questions.title: delete_contact: 8% Reversals Due to Misidentified Records (Similar Names)
questions.text: Your CRM agent's delete_contact tool handles requests like "delete the duplicate entry for Acme Corp." The database contains similarly named records (e.g., "Acme Corp," "Acme Corporation," "ACME Corp Inc."), and analytics show 8% of deletions are reversed within 24 hours due to misidentified records. Users have also complained that the current multi-step confirmation flow adds too much friction to routine cleanup tasks. Which approach most effectively reduces the error rate while maintaining workflow efficiency?

options.A: Present matched records with differentiating fields and require single-click confirmation of the intended target before executing deletion.
options.B: Deploy automated duplicate detection that identifies and merges probable duplicates, removing the need for manual deletion requests.
options.C: Implement soft-delete with a 30-day recovery window so users can undo mistakes without slowing down the deletion workflow.
options.D: Require users to supply the exact record ID from the CRM interface rather than using natural language references to contact names.

questions.explanation:
Why A: This directly addresses the root cause (misidentification from similar names) by surfacing differentiating
information before confirming, while keeping the workflow to a single confirmation click.

optionExplanations.A: This directly addresses the root cause (misidentification from similar names) by surfacing differentiating information before confirming, while keeping the workflow to a single confirmation click.
optionExplanations.B: Automated merging without user review bypasses the core problem—users need to confirm they're deleting the *right* record, not have a system decide which duplicates belong together.
optionExplanations.C: Soft-delete addresses data recovery but does nothing to reduce the error rate of misidentifying which record to delete in the first place.
optionExplanations.D: Requiring record IDs increases friction rather than reducing it, working against the stated goal of maintaining efficiency while lowering errors.

questions.title: send_notification Timeouts: Cannot Determine If Message Was Sent
questions.text: Your send_notification tool calls third-party messaging APIs. When these services time out during delivery, you cannot determine whether the message was actually sent. Currently, the tool returns is_error: true with a generic "Notification failed" message for all timeouts. Production monitoring reveals agents automatically retry these failures, frequently causing users to receive duplicate notifications. How should you modify the error response?

options.A: Return is_error: true with a structured field retry_safe: true for timeouts, distinguishing them from permanent failures that should not be retried.
options.B: Return is_error: false with the original message content echoed back.
options.C: Return is_error: true with a message encouraging retry: "Delivery service temporarily unavailable. Please retry the notification."
options.D: Return is_error: true with a message communicating uncertainty: "Timeout—status unknown. Message may have been sent. Avoid retry."

questions.explanation:
Why D: The critical information is the delivery uncertainty and the instruction NOT to retry. Option A with
retry_safe: true would cause the exact duplicate problem you're trying to prevent.

optionExplanations.A: With retry_safe: true would cause the exact duplicate problem you're trying to prevent.
optionExplanations.B: Returning is_error: false would mask the actual failure and mislead the agent into thinking the message was successfully sent, perpetuating the duplicate notification problem.
optionExplanations.C: Encouraging retry on a timeout (where delivery status is unknown) directly causes the duplicate notifications you're trying to prevent.
optionExplanations.D: The critical information is the delivery uncertainty and the instruction NOT to retry

questions.title: send_notification Timeouts: Cannot Determine If Message Was Sent
questions.text: Your send_notification tool calls third-party messaging APIs. When these services time out during delivery, you cannot determine whether the message was actually sent. Currently, the tool returns is_error: true with a generic "Notification failed" message for all timeouts. Production monitoring reveals agents automatically retry these failures, frequently causing users to receive duplicate notifications. How should you modify the error response?

options.A: Return is_error: true with a structured field retry_safe: true for timeouts, distinguishing them from permanent failures that should not be retried.
options.B: Return is_error: false with the original message content echoed back.
options.C: Return is_error: true with a message encouraging retry: "Delivery service temporarily unavailable. Please retry the notification."
options.D: Return is_error: true with a message communicating uncertainty: "Timeout—status unknown. Message may have been sent. Avoid retry."

questions.explanation: Why D: The critical information is the delivery uncertainty and the instruction NOT to retry. Option A with retry_safe: true would cause the exact duplicate problem you're trying to prevent.

optionExplanations.A: With retry_safe: true this would cause the exact duplicate problem you're trying to prevent—timeouts leave delivery status unknown, so retrying can send the message twice.
optionExplanations.B: Returning is_error: false would mask the actual failure and mislead the agent into thinking the message was successfully sent, perpetuating the duplicate notification problem.
optionExplanations.C: Encouraging retry on a timeout (where delivery status is unknown) directly causes the duplicate notifications you're trying to prevent.
optionExplanations.D: The critical information is the delivery uncertainty and the instruction NOT to retry. Option A with retry_safe: true would cause the exact duplicate problem you're trying to prevent.

questions.title: search_flights Tool: 503 Service Unavailable from External Airline API
questions.text: Your search_flights tool calls an external airline API that occasionally returns a 503 Service Unavailable error. What is the most effective way to handle this error in your tool implementation?

options.A: Return an error message in the tool result explaining the service is temporarily unavailable.
options.B: Log the error internally and return an empty response, letting the model continue without the flight data.
options.C: Return an empty flight list as if the search succeeded but found no matching flights.
options.D: Automatically retry the request up to five times with exponential backoff before returning results to the agent.

questions.explanation: Why A: Returning a clear error message (with isError: true) lets the agent understand the failure and communicate it to the user. An empty response (B) is ambiguous. An empty results list (C) misleads the agent. Auto-retrying 5 times (D) could add tens of seconds of latency to every 503 error.

optionExplanations.A: Returning a clear error message (with isError: true) lets the agent understand the failure and communicate it to the user. An empty response (B) is ambiguous. An empty results list (C) misleads the agent. Auto-retrying 5 times (D) could add tens of seconds of latency to every 503 error.
optionExplanations.B: An empty response is ambiguous—the agent cannot distinguish between a genuine empty result and a failure, leaving it unable to communicate the actual problem to the user.
optionExplanations.C: Returning an empty flight list falsely signals success, causing the agent to mislead the user into thinking no flights exist rather than revealing the service failure.
optionExplanations.D: Five retries with exponential backoff can add 30+ seconds of latency to every transient error, severely degrading user experience when the service is temporarily down.

questions.title: process_reimbursement: $500 Approval Threshold Must Be Tamper-Proof
questions.text: Your expense reimbursement agent processes employee requests using a process_reimbursement tool. Company policy requires that reimbursements above $500 must be approved by a manager before funds are disbursed. The agent handles hundreds of requests daily, and you need the threshold enforcement to be tamper-proof regardless of how the agent is prompted. Which design ensures the $500 approval threshold cannot be bypassed?

options.A: Provide two tools: auto_reimburse (hard-coded limit of $500) and request_manager_approval. Include detailed system prompt instructions telling the agent to check the amount and call the appropriate tool. Add a PostToolUse hook that logs which tool was called for auditing.
options.B: The process_reimbursement tool accepts an approved_by_manager: boolean parameter. The system prompt instructs the agent to only set this to true after confirming that a manager has approved the request. A nightly audit script reviews all reimbursements where approved_by_manager was set to true.
options.C: The process_reimbursement tool accepts amount and details, and internally enforces the threshold: amounts <=$500 are auto-disbursed and the tool returns a success confirmation; amounts >$500 cause the tool to create a pending approval request and return a status indicating manager review is pending.
options.D: Implement the threshold check in a PreToolUse hook that inspects the amount parameter before process_reimbursement executes. If the amount exceeds $500, the hook modifies the tool call to add a requires_approval: true flag, which the tool checks before disbursing.

questions.explanation: Why C: The business rule is enforced at the tool implementation level, completely independent of the agent's behavior. The agent cannot bypass it regardless of how it is prompted.

optionExplanations.A: Relying on system prompt instructions to control agent behavior is insufficient—the agent can be prompted to ignore or override those instructions, leaving the threshold bypassable.
optionExplanations.B: This depends on the agent following the boolean parameter convention correctly; a prompt injection or reframing could cause the agent to set approved_by_manager to true without actual approval.
optionExplanations.C: The business rule is enforced at the tool implementation level, completely independent of the agent's behavior. The agent cannot bypass it regardless of how it is prompted.
optionExplanations.D: A hook that modifies the tool call after the agent has already decided to invoke it cannot prevent the agent from crafting the initial request—it only catches and corrects it, which is reactive rather than structurally impossible.

questions.title: log_workout: 23% Invalid Measurement Combinations
questions.text: Your agent has a log_workout tool that accepts exercise_type (string), value (number), and measurement (string). Production monitoring shows the agent frequently passes mismatched combinations—using measurement: "reps" for cardio exercises like running, or measurement: "miles" for strength exercises like bench press. Your exercises naturally divide into two categories: cardio (measured in time or distance) and strength (measured in reps and sets). 23% of tool calls have invalid combinations. What approach would most effectively reduce these errors?

options.A: Add enum constraints on measurement limiting values to "minutes", "miles", "reps", or "sets" to prevent arbitrary measurement strings.
options.B: Split into log_cardio_workout (with duration_minutes or distance_miles parameters) and log_strength_workout (with reps and sets parameters).
options.C: Add explicit examples to the tool description showing valid combinations (e.g., "For running: use minutes or miles. For push-ups: use reps") with constraints for each exercise category.
options.D: Implement server-side validation returning descriptive errors for invalid combinations, allowing the agent to retry with corrections.

questions.explanation: Why B: Splitting tools by exercise type makes invalid combinations structurally impossible. The correct parameters are enforced by the schema itself—no documentation, examples, or validation needed.

optionExplanations.A: Enums constrain the values themselves but don't solve the mapping problem—the agent still needs to choose which measurement type to use for a given exercise, leaving room for incorrect pairings.
optionExplanations.B: Splitting tools by exercise type makes invalid combinations structurally impossible. The correct parameters are enforced by the schema itself—no documentation, examples, or validation needed.
optionExplanations.C: Examples in tool descriptions require the agent to read, understand, and follow them correctly; they provide guidance but don't prevent the agent from ignoring them and using the wrong measurement type.
optionExplanations.D: Server-side validation only catches errors after the agent has already made a mistake; it requires the agent to iterate and retry rather than making the incorrect choice impossible from the start.

questions.title: Scheduling Agent: 15% "Slot No Longer Available" Failures (Race Condition)
questions.text: Your scheduling agent uses get_available_slots(date, provider_id) to retrieve open appointment times, then book_appointment(provider_id, slot_time, patient_id) to reserve a slot. Support tickets show that 15% of booking attempts fail with "slot no longer available" because another user booked the slot between the availability check and the booking call. How should you redesign these tools?

options.A: Modify book_appointment to return detailed failure information including currently available alternative slots when the requested slot is unavailable, enabling the agent to retry with a different time.
options.B: Combine both tools into a single find_and_book_appointment that atomically checks availability and books, returning either the confirmed booking or available alternatives.
options.C: Add a hold_slot(provider_id, slot_time) tool that creates a 60-second temporary reservation, requiring the agent to call it between checking availability and booking.
options.D: Keep both tools but add retry logic to the agent's system prompt, instructing it to call get_available_slots again and select a different time if booking fails.

questions.explanation: Why C: This directly solves the race condition by creating a temporary hold. The workflow becomes: get_available_slots → hold_slot → show options → book_appointment, with the held slot protected from concurrent booking.

optionExplanations.A: Returning alternative slots only helps if the agent acts on them immediately, but doesn't prevent another user from booking those alternatives in the time it takes the agent to process the response.
optionExplanations.B: While atomicity solves the race condition, combining tools removes the agent's ability to show users available options before committing to a booking, making the user experience less flexible.
optionExplanations.C: This directly solves the race condition by creating a temporary hold. The workflow becomes: get_available_slots → hold_slot → show options → book_appointment, with the held slot protected from concurrent booking.
optionExplanations.D: Retry logic in the system prompt is agent-behavior guidance that can be ignored or mishandled; it doesn't address the underlying race condition—a third party could still book during the retry loop.

questions.title: Document Extraction Confidence Scores: Two Problems
questions.text: Your document extraction tool uses ML models to extract invoice fields (vendor, amount, date). The models return confidence scores (0.0-1.0) for each extracted field. In production, you observe: (1) the agent proceeds with low-confidence extractions that are incorrect 23% of the time, and (2) the agent requests unnecessary human review for 31% of extractions that were actually correct. How should you restructure the tool's output?

options.A: Compute an aggregate extraction_quality score across all fields and return it alongside the extracted values. Include a text summary describing the overall extraction reliability.
options.B: Return fields with their raw confidence scores and add detailed few-shot examples to your system prompt demonstrating how to interpret different confidence ranges and when to request human review.
options.C: Return fields organized into verified and needs_verification objects based on confidence thresholds.
options.D: Return fields with confidence scores, plus a requires_review boolean computed using your tested confidence thresholds, along with a review_reasons array explaining which fields triggered review.

questions.explanation:
Why D: The requires_review boolean (computed from calibrated, tested thresholds) directly solves both
problems: it stops unnecessary reviews for correctly-extracted high-confidence fields, and it flags genuinely
uncertain low-confidence fields. The review_reasons array helps human reviewers focus their attention.

optionExplanations.A: An aggregate score obscures which specific fields are problematic, making it impossible for the agent to distinguish between high-confidence and low-confidence extractions or decide which fields genuinely need review.
optionExplanations.B: Raw confidence scores alone shift the interpretation burden to the agent, which has already proven unreliable at making the right judgment calls (proceeding incorrectly 23% of the time and over-requesting review 31% of the time).
optionExplanations.C: Pre-sorting fields into verified and needs_verification categories based on generic thresholds doesn't account for the agent's actual error patterns and cannot incorporate the tested calibration that fixes both over-confidence and over-caution.
optionExplanations.D: The requires_review boolean (computed from calibrated, tested thresholds) directly solves both problems: it stops unnecessary reviews for correctly-extracted high-confidence fields, and it flags genuinely uncertain low-confidence fields. The review_reasons array helps human reviewers focus their attention.
